div.p: When r/m = 100 and mod ≠ 11, a SIB byte follows: Scale (bits 7-6): 1, 2, 4, or 8 Index (bits 5-3): register × scale Base (bits 2-0): base register Example: [rbp + rax*4 + 16] = SIB with scale=4, index=rax, base=rbp
r/m field (columns) vs mod field (rows) (For 64-bit mode with REX.W prefix)

text.tspan: ; reg=011 (rbx in reg field as opcode ext)
; Wait - let me fix: reg=000 (rax), r/m=011 (rbx)
; reg=011 (rbx in reg field as opcode ext)
; Wait - let me fix: reg=000 (rax), r/m=011 (rbx)

div.p: In 64-bit mode, this encodes RIP-relative addressing: [rip + disp32] Used for position-independent code and accessing global variables.
When r/m = 100 and mod ≠ 11, a SIB byte MUST follow. This allows complex addressing: [base + index × scale + disp] rsp cannot be a base alone (would conflict with SIB marker).
REX.R extends reg field → r8-r15 REX.B extends r/m field → r8-r15 REX.X extends SIB index → r8-r15 Without REX: registers 0-7 With REX: registers 0-15
